mn19:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn19:11.1: Whatever, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* frequently thinks [*bahulamanuvitakketi*] and examines [*anuvicāreti*], in that way the mind inclines.
mn19:11.2: If a *bhikkhu* frequently thinks [*bahulamanuvitakketi*] and examines [*anuvicāreti*] a thought of renunciation [*nekkhammavitakkaṁ*], he has abandoned the thought of sensuality [*kāmavitakkaṁ*], made the thought of renunciation [*nekkhammavitakkaṁ*] frequent, and his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to a thought of renunciation [*nekkhammavitakkāya*].
mn19:11.3: If a *bhikkhu* frequently thinks [*bahulamanuvitakketi*] and examines [*anuvicāreti*] a thought of non-ill will [*abyāpādavitakkaṁ*] …
mn19:11.4: If a *bhikkhu* frequently thinks [*bahulamanuvitakketi*] and examines [*anuvicāreti*] a thought of non-harming [*avihiṁsāvitakkaṁ*], he has abandoned the thought of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakkaṁ*], made the thought of non-harming [*avihiṁsāvitakkaṁ*] frequent, and his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to a thought of non-harming [*avihiṁsāvitakkāya*].
mn19:12.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, in the last month of the hot season, when all the crops have been gathered and stored near the village, a cowherd were guarding cows. Whether at the foot of a tree or out in the open, he would simply need to keep in mind:
mn19:12.3: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, all that I had to keep in mind was:
mn19:13.1: But my energy was aroused and unflagging, *sati* was established and not forgotten, my body was tranquil and unagitated, my mind [*cittaṁ*] endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*], gathered [*ekaggaṁ*].
mn19:14.1: Quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas*, I entered and dwelt in the first *jhāna*, with thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*].
mn19:15.1: With the settling of thought and examination [*vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā*], I entered and dwelt in the second *jhāna*, with internal confidence, unified [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] mind, without thought [*avitakkaṁ*] and without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijaṁ*].
mn19:16.1: And with the fading of *pīti*, I dwelt in equanimity [*upekkhā*], with *sati* and with full awareness [*sampajāno*], and I felt *sukha* with the body. I entered and dwelt in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones say: ‘One who is equanimous [*upekkhako*], with *sati* [*satimā*], dwells in *sukha*.’
mn19:17.1: With the giving up of *sukha* and the giving up of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and grief, I entered and dwelt in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither painful nor pleasant, purified by equanimity and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*].
mn19:18.1: When the mind [*citta*] was thus endowed with *samādhi*, purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and imperturbable, I inclined it to the knowledge of recollecting former lives.
mn19:18.2: I recollect my manifold former lives, that is: one birth … Thus, with features and details, I recollect my manifold former lives.
mn19:19.1: This, *bhikkhus*, was the first true knowledge attained by me in the first watch of the night.
mn19:19.2: Blindness [*avijjā*] was dispelled, true knowledge arose; darkness was dispelled, light arose—as happens for one dwelling diligent, ardent, and resolute.
mn19:2.1: “Before my awakening, *bhikkhus*, when I was not yet fully awakened, while I was still a *bodhisatta*, this occurred to me:
mn19:2.2: ‘Why don’t I dwell having divided thoughts [*vitakke*] into two kinds?’
mn19:2.3: So, *bhikkhus*, whatever is a thought of sensuality [*kāmavitakko*], a thought of ill will [*byāpādavitakko*], or a thought of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakko*]—
mn19:2.5: And whatever is a thought of renunciation [*nekkhammavitakko*], a thought of non-ill will [*abyāpādavitakko*], or a thought of non-harming [*avihiṁsāvitakko*]—
mn19:2.6: I made that the second part.
mn19:20.1: When the mind [*citta*] was thus endowed with *samādhi*, purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and imperturbable, I inclined it to the knowledge of the passing away and reappearance of beings.
mn19:20.2: With the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, I see beings passing away and reappearing … ‘These worthy beings were engaged in bodily misconduct …’ Thus with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, I see beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good destination and a bad destination; I understand beings as going according to their kamma.
mn19:21.1: This, *bhikkhus*, was the second true knowledge attained by me in the middle watch of the night.
mn19:21.2: Blindness [*avijjā*] was dispelled, true knowledge arose; darkness was dispelled, light arose—as happens for one dwelling diligent, ardent, and resolute.
mn19:22.1: When the mind [*citta*] was thus endowed with *samādhi*, purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and imperturbable, I inclined it to the knowledge of the ending of the *āsavas*.
mn19:22.2: I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is *dukkha*.’ I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*.’ I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*.’ I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn19:23.1: I directly knew as it truly is: ‘These are the *āsavas*.’ I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of the *āsavas*.’ I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of the *āsavas*.’ I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of the *āsavas*.’
mn19:23.2: For me, knowing and seeing thus, the mind [*cittaṁ*] was liberated from the *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavā*], from the *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavā*], and from the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavā*]. When it was liberated, there was the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn19:23.3: I directly knew: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn19:24.1: This, *bhikkhus*, was the third true knowledge attained by me in the last watch of the night.
mn19:24.2: Blindness [*avijjā*] was dispelled, true knowledge arose; darkness was dispelled, light arose—as happens for one dwelling diligent, ardent, and resolute.
mn19:25.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, in a forest wilderness there were a great low-lying marsh.
mn19:25.2: A large herd of deer might live depending on it.
mn19:25.3: Then some person might appear who wanted their harm, wanted their hurt, wanted their insecurity from bondage.
mn19:25.4: He would block the path that was safe, auspicious, and led to happiness; he would open a wrong path, set a bait, and post a decoy.
mn19:25.5: In that way, *bhikkhus*, after some time that large herd of deer would come to ruin and disaster.
mn19:25.6: But then another person might appear for the welfare of that very large herd of deer, wanting their benefit, wanting their good, wanting their security from bondage.
mn19:25.7: He would open the path that was safe, auspicious, and led to happiness; he would block the wrong path, remove the bait, and destroy the decoy.
mn19:25.8: In that way, *bhikkhus*, after some time that large herd of deer would come to growth, increase, and abundance.
mn19:26.1: This simile, *bhikkhus*, has been made by me in order to make the meaning understood.
mn19:26.10: ‘A person who wants benefit, wants good, wants security from bondage,’ *bhikkhus*, is a designation for the Tathāgata, the arahant, the perfectly awakened one.
mn19:26.11: ‘The safe path, the auspicious path, the path leading to happiness,’ *bhikkhus*, is a designation for this noble eightfold path, that is:
mn19:26.12: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.
mn19:26.13: So, *bhikkhus*, the safe path, the auspicious path leading to happiness, has been opened by me; the wrong path has been closed off; the bait has been removed; the decoy has been destroyed.
mn19:26.2: And this is the meaning here:
mn19:26.3: ‘A great low-lying marsh,’ *bhikkhus*, is a designation for sensual pleasures.
mn19:26.4: ‘A large herd of deer,’ *bhikkhus*, is a designation for beings.
mn19:26.5: ‘A person who wants harm, wants hurt, wants insecurity from bondage,’ *bhikkhus*, is a designation for Māra the Evil One.
mn19:26.6: ‘A wrong path,’ *bhikkhus*, is a designation for this wrong eightfold path, that is:
mn19:26.7: wrong view, wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong *sati*, wrong *samādhi*.
mn19:26.8: ‘Bait,’ *bhikkhus*, is a designation for delight and lust.
mn19:27.1: What should be done by a teacher for disciples, out of compassion, seeking their welfare and taking pity on them—that has been done for you by me.
mn19:27.2: These are the roots of trees, *bhikkhus*; these are empty huts. Meditate [*jhāyatha*], *bhikkhus*; do not be negligent; do not later be remorseful. This is our instruction to you.”
mn19:27.4: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn19:27.5: The ninth: Two Kinds of Thought is finished.
mn19:3.1: While I dwelt diligent, ardent, and resolute, *bhikkhus*, a thought of sensuality [*kāmavitakko*] arose in me.
mn19:3.3: ‘This thought of sensuality [*kāmavitakko*] has arisen in me.
mn19:3.4: And it leads to my own affliction, to others’ affliction, and to the affliction of both; it obstructs discernment [*paññānirodhiko*], sides with distress [*vighātapakkhiko*], and does not lead to *nibbāna* [*anibbānasaṁvattaniko*].’
mn19:3.5: When I reflected, *bhikkhus*, ‘It leads to my own affliction,’ it vanished.
mn19:3.6: When I reflected, *bhikkhus*, ‘It leads to others’ affliction,’ it vanished.
mn19:3.7: When I reflected, *bhikkhus*, ‘It leads to the affliction of both,’ it vanished.
mn19:3.8: When I reflected, *bhikkhus*, ‘It obstructs discernment [*paññānirodhiko*], sides with distress [*vighātapakkhiko*], and does not lead to *nibbāna* [*anibbānasaṁvattaniko*],’ it vanished.
mn19:3.9: So, *bhikkhus*, whenever a thought of sensuality [*kāmavitakkaṁ*] arose, I abandoned it, dispelled it, and put an end to it.
mn19:4-5.1: While I dwelt diligent, ardent, and resolute, *bhikkhus*, a thought of ill will [*byāpādavitakko*] arose in me … a thought of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakko*] arose in me.
mn19:4-5.10: So, *bhikkhus*, whenever a thought of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakkaṁ*] arose, I abandoned it, dispelled it, and put an end to it.
mn19:4-5.4: ‘This thought of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakko*] has arisen in me.
mn19:4-5.5: And it leads to my own affliction, to others’ affliction, and to the affliction of both; it obstructs discernment [*paññānirodhiko*], sides with distress [*vighātapakkhiko*], and does not lead to *nibbāna* [*anibbānasaṁvattaniko*].’
mn19:4-5.6: When I reflected, *bhikkhus*, ‘It leads to my own affliction,’ it vanished.
mn19:4-5.7: When I reflected, *bhikkhus*, ‘It leads to others’ affliction,’ it vanished.
mn19:4-5.8: When I reflected, *bhikkhus*, ‘It leads to the affliction of both,’ it vanished.
mn19:4-5.9: When I reflected, *bhikkhus*, ‘It obstructs discernment [*paññānirodhiko*], sides with distress [*vighātapakkhiko*], and does not lead to *nibbāna* [*anibbānasaṁvattaniko*],’ it vanished.
mn19:6.1: Whatever, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* frequently thinks [*bahulamanuvitakketi*] and examines [*anuvicāreti*], in that way the mind inclines.
mn19:6.2: If a *bhikkhu* frequently thinks [*bahulamanuvitakketi*] and examines [*anuvicāreti*] a thought of sensuality [*kāmavitakkaṁ*], he has abandoned the thought of renunciation [*nekkhammavitakkaṁ*], made the thought of sensuality [*kāmavitakkaṁ*] frequent, and his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to a thought of sensuality [*kāmavitakkāya*].
mn19:6.3: If a *bhikkhu* frequently thinks [*bahulamanuvitakketi*] and examines [*anuvicāreti*] a thought of ill will [*byāpādavitakkaṁ*] …
mn19:6.4: If a *bhikkhu* frequently thinks [*bahulamanuvitakketi*] and examines [*anuvicāreti*] a thought of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakkaṁ*], he has abandoned the thought of non-harming [*avihiṁsāvitakkaṁ*], made the thought of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakkaṁ*] frequent, and his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to a thought of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakkāya*].
mn19:7.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, in the last month of the rains, in the autumn season, when the crops are thick in the fields, a cowherd were guarding cows.
mn19:7.2: He would strike those cows here and there with a stick, check them, hold them back, and keep them away.
mn19:7.4: Because that cowherd sees that from that there could come a beating, imprisonment, a fine, or blame.
mn19:7.5: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, I saw the drawback, the degradation, and the defilement in unwholesome *dhammas*, and the benefit in renunciation, the side of cleansing, in wholesome *dhammas*.
mn19:8.1: While I dwelt diligent, ardent, and resolute, *bhikkhus*, a thought of renunciation [*nekkhammavitakko*] arose in me.
mn19:8.10: When the mind [*citta*] is strained, it is far from *samādhi*.
mn19:8.11: So, *bhikkhus*, I steady the mind [*cittaṁ*] inwardly, settle it, make it unified [*ekodiṁ*], and place it in *samādhi*.
mn19:8.13: ‘So that my mind [*cittaṁ*] may not become strained.’
mn19:8.3: ‘This thought of renunciation [*nekkhammavitakko*] has arisen in me.
mn19:8.4: And it does not lead to my own affliction, nor to others’ affliction, nor to the affliction of both; it promotes discernment [*paññāvuddhiko*], sides with non-distress [*avighātapakkhiko*], and leads to *nibbāna* [*nibbānasaṁvattaniko*].’
mn19:8.5: Even if I were to keep thinking [*anuvitakkeyyaṁ*] and examining [*anuvicāreyyaṁ*] it by night, *bhikkhus*, I see no danger from that.
mn19:8.6: Even if I were to keep thinking [*anuvitakkeyyaṁ*] and examining [*anuvicāreyyaṁ*] it by day, *bhikkhus*, I see no danger from that.
mn19:8.7: Even if I were to keep thinking [*anuvitakkeyyaṁ*] and examining [*anuvicāreyyaṁ*] it by night and day, *bhikkhus*, I see no danger from that.
mn19:8.8: But if I were to keep thinking [*anuvitakkayato*] and examining [*anuvicārayato*] too long, my body might become tired.
mn19:8.9: When the body is tired, the mind [*cittaṁ*] becomes strained.
mn19:9-10.1: While I dwelt diligent, ardent, and resolute, *bhikkhus*, a thought of non-ill will [*abyāpādavitakko*] arose in me … a thought of non-harming [*avihiṁsāvitakko*] arose in me.
mn19:9-10.10: When the body is tired, the mind [*cittaṁ*] becomes strained.
mn19:9-10.11: When the mind [*citta*] is strained, it is far from *samādhi*.
mn19:9-10.12: So, *bhikkhus*, I steady the mind [*cittaṁ*] inwardly, settle it, make it unified [*ekodiṁ*], and place it in *samādhi*.
mn19:9-10.14: ‘So that my mind [*cittaṁ*] may not become strained.’
mn19:9-10.4: ‘This thought of non-harming [*avihiṁsāvitakko*] has arisen in me.
mn19:9-10.5: And it does not lead to my own affliction, nor to others’ affliction, nor to the affliction of both; it promotes discernment [*paññāvuddhiko*], sides with non-distress [*avighātapakkhiko*], and leads to *nibbāna* [*nibbānasaṁvattaniko*].’
mn19:9-10.6: Even if I were to keep thinking [*anuvitakkeyyaṁ*] and examining [*anuvicāreyyaṁ*] it by night, *bhikkhus*, I see no danger from that.
mn19:9-10.7: Even if I were to keep thinking [*anuvitakkeyyaṁ*] and examining [*anuvicāreyyaṁ*] it by day, *bhikkhus*, I see no danger from that.
mn19:9-10.8: Even if I were to keep thinking [*anuvitakkeyyaṁ*] and examining [*anuvicāreyyaṁ*] it by night and day, *bhikkhus*, I see no danger from that.
mn19:9-10.9: But if I were to keep thinking [*anuvitakkayato*] and examining [*anuvicārayato*] too long, my body might become tired.
